column2: SQUIP: Exploiting the Scheduler Queue Contention Side Channel
column5: No evaluation for this conference this year

column20: Compare to the numbers in previous papers

column24:
Zeroes are encoded as multiple series of 15 nops, leading to low pressure on the ALU1 scheduler, while ones are multiple series of 15 dependent imuls, leading to high pressure. The exact number of series is dependent on how fast nops and imuls are executed, thus dependent on the micro-architecture.
The receiver measures the time needed to perform 16 (Zen 2) or 18 (Zen 3) dependent multiplications.
Receiver and sender are not synchronized. A preamble packet is sent allowing the receiver to evaluate the length in samples of a single bit. A trailer packet marks the end of the message.
column55: Crypto - CF-based (e.g. mod exp / ECDSA / ECSM) - known vulnerability
column57:
Attack performed on a sliding window Montgomery modular exponentiation algorithm, where a square operation followed by a multiply induces more contention on the ALU1 scheduler. Authors performed the attack both a cross-process and a cross-VM setup where attacker and victim are co-located on the same physical core.
10 contention profiles are collected with 5050 traces each, the best one is selected for key extraction.

column2: Binoculars: Contention-Based Side-Channel Attacks Exploiting the Page Walker
column5: Evaluation was available, but the paper didn't do/get it

column24: Sender and receiver are assumed to have agreed on a specific offset. No details are given regarding how synchronization is achieved. To send a 1, sender writes repeatedly to that offset, and does nothing to send a 0. The receiver meanwhile issues a TLB-missing read to a target page where the page walk induces reads 4K-aliased with the sender's writes. If the receiver measures a high latency on this read, the sender is sending 1, 0 otherwise.
column55: Crypto - CF-based (e.g. mod exp / ECDSA / ECSM) - known vulnerability
column57: The authors use their attack to identify when a specific store S in the Montgomery ladder algorithm is executed, which reveals the bit value of the nonce k. In an offline phase, the attacker first records page offsets of stores executed during the Montgomery ladder, and identify those corresponding to S. They also build a random forest classifier to predict boundaries between each loop iteration and the values of k. The attacker then repeatedly times 4 dependent TLB-missing load whose page walks produce reads aliased with S, and uses this as input for the classifier. For bits where the classifier outputs a low-confidence prediction, brute-forcing is used.
column58: 130 traces for training, a single trace is enough to recover k in 77.9% of cases with reasonable brute-forcing
column61: Authors re-evaluated the attack to compare on the same conditions
column66: Ubuntu 20.04 LTS with the kernel version 5.4.0-105-generic
column67: The authors attack a system call that accesses a global variable whose offset within the kernel is known: the SYS_time system call. By flushing the TLB, the syscall will issue a page walk which will conflict with 4K-aliased writes by the attacker, slowing down the syscall. The attacker, by repeating the operation for every possible offset and by timing the syscall, can retrieve the virtual page number of this memory access.

column2: Lord of the Ring(s): Side Channel Attacks on the CPU On-Chip Ring Interconnect Are Practical
column5: Yes
column16: Covert channel;Side channel (includes (K)ASLR break, crypto attack, ...)
column17: Speed;Multiple plateforms;Capacity (bandwith considering the error rate);Transient execution
column24: The sender and the receiver do not need to share the cache. The basic idea of the sender is to transmit a bit “1” by creating contention on the ring interconnect and a bit “0” by idling, thus creating no ring contention. Simultaneously, the receiver times loads that travel through a segment of the ring interconnect susceptible to contention due to the sender’s loads.Therefore, when the sender is sending a “1”, the receiver experiences delays in its load latency. To distinguish a “0” from a “1” the receiver can use the mean load latency: smaller load latencies are assigned to a “0”, and larger load latencies are assigned to a “1”.

column2: Hertzbleed: Turning Power Side-Channel Attacks Into Remote Timing Attacks on x86
column5: Yes
column16: Covert channel;Side channel (includes (K)ASLR break, crypto attack, ...)
column24:
Their covert channel uses a sender and a receiver. 
To transmit a 0, the sender executes a loop of or instructions with high Hamming Distance (HD) and Hamming Weight (HW) in their data flow. This loop increases the power consumption and results in lower CPU frequency values.
To transmit a 1, the sender executes a loop of shlx instructions with low HD and HW in their data flow. This loop decreases the power consumption and results in higher CPU frequency values.
The receiver measures the current CPU frequency using the unprivileged scaling_cur_freq interface from the cpufreq driver. 
They evaluated their covert channel by transmitting 1 kB of random data on the intel i7-9700 CPU.

column2: Last-Level Cache Side-Channel Attacks are Practical
column5: No evaluation for this conference this year

column24:
First the sender and the receiver each allocate a buffer of at least the size of the LLC, backed up by large pages. 
Then, they both agree on two arbitrarily chosen cache-set indices (preferably without interferences from non-participating memory accesses).
After that, the sender picks from its allocated buffer two memory lines, line 0 and line 1, that map to the two agreed cache set indices. To send a “1”, the sender continuously accesses line 1 for an amount of time t. Similarly, the sender continuously accesses line 0 for a time equal to t to send a “0”.
The return-to-zero self-clocking encoding scheme makes sure there is enough time between two consecutive bits by busy waiting for a certain amount of time. Before monitoring the sender, the receiver first uses the algorithm 1 from the paper to create eviction sets set 1 and set 0 for line 1 and line 0. It then uses Prime+Probe to continuously monitor the two sets.
column26: They conducted an experiment to measure the effect of the pause duration on channel capacity and error rate. They found that increasing the pause duration reduces the error rate but also the channel capacity.

column2: Augury: Using Data Memory-Dependent Prefetchers to Leak Data at Rest
column5: No evaluation for this conference this year
column16: Side channel (includes (K)ASLR break, crypto attack, ...)
column55: Toy example / Microbenchmark
column57: The toy example demonstrates how the DMP can be used to read values past the end of an array, similarly to the original Spectre attack. The array is initialized to contain random unique pointers. The author train the DMP by streaming through this array and dereferencing its pointers. This causes the DMP to prefetch the first cache line after the end of the array, interpret it as a pointer, then prefetch that value. This attack assumes the adversary can access these pointers, measuring the latency.
column67: The toy example demonstrates how the DMP can be used to read values past the end of an array, similarly to the original Spectre attack. The array is initialized to contain random unique pointers. The author train the DMP by streaming through this array and dereferencing its pointers. This causes the DMP to prefetch the first cache line after the end of the array, interpret it as a pointer, then prefetch that value. The presence of this value in the cache is checked by the attacker using Prime+Probe.
column77: The confidence threshold of the prefetcher is used to test the validity of a virtual address. After the first and second accesses, the prefetcher is activated and will prefetch the third element of the array containing the target address. It will then attempt to dereference that address. If it is valid, the prefetcher will continue similarly with the fourth element of the array. If it is not, the prefetcher will have low confidence and stop issuing predictions. The attacker can then time the access latency of the fourth element to determine if the target address was valid.

column24:
Two variants are described, corresponding to the two primitives introduced.
1) ActiveIdleLeak: the receiver repeatedly measures the timing of a nop series. To send a 0 the sender enters idle state C0.2 via instruction tpause, the receiver measures the increased performance of the nop series. To send a 1 the sender enters a busy wait loop instead.
2) PassiveIdleLeak: the receiver repeatedly enters idle state C0.2 and measure its wakeup time. To send a 0 the sender generates div-by-zero exception, leading to the sender being woken up more often. To send a 1 the sender instead does nothing.
Synchronization: on a local setup a previously agreed upon TSC value is used, while on a VM setup an alternating sequence of 1 and 0 is used to signal the start of the transmission.

For evaluation, the authors consider both a closed-world and an open-world scenario (where the classifier will be presented with traces corresponding to websites not present in the training set). column24:
The covert channel aims to transmit 8 bits of information in 3 steps.
1. The spy triggers a DMA to initiate the transfer of N bytes of data from a shared memory bank and starts a timer.
2. Then it invokes a malicious code (a trojan) injected into the victim domain, which generates a specific number of memory accesses, depending on the secret it wants to transmit.
3. Lastly, the spy reads the timer, measuring the time it took for the DMA to transfer all the N bytes of data.
column55: Crypto - memory access-based (e.g. AES T-table) - known vulnerability
column57:
The attack has two phases:

- the profiling phase, the attacker uses a profiling-SGN to capture the memory access patterns. This phase has three steps. 
1. the spy instructs the SGN to generate contention at a specific clock cycle.
2. the spy invokes the victim b.
3. after a predetermined amount of time has elapsed, the trigger contention gadget activates the record contention gadget. This process is repeated (loop) until the victim executes the last instruction. The profiling is conducted for each of the 17 potential execution paths in the read_keypad code (16 distinct keys plus one path for the absence of key press). Each execution path may generate one of two patterns: one when a key is pressed, and another when there is no key press.

- the exploitation phase, the attacker uses the exploitation-SGN to retrieve the secret.
=> They successfully bypassed all isolation barriers at PSA level 2 and 3 (TrustZone and secure MPU) and retrieved the secret of the smart lock TA with a 100% success rate, i.e., they always recover the user’s PIN. (Part less clear than the previous one)

column55: (Inter)keystroke recovery
column57:
To run the side-channel attack on a private memory address t in a victim process, the attacker allocates a memory address p that maps to the same bank and the same row as the target address t. Although t and p map to the same DRAM row, they belong to different 4 KB pages (i.e., no shared memory). The attacker also allocates a row conflict address p ̄  that maps to the same bank but to a different row.

The side-channel attack then works in three steps:
1. Access the row conflict address p ̄
2. Wait for the victim to compute
3. Measure the access time on the targeted address p

If the measured timing is below a row-hit threshold, the victim has just accessed t or another address in the target row. Thus, it is possible to determine when a specific non-shared memory location is accessed by a process running on another core or CPU.

In their first experiment, the victim performs a secret-dependent  branch within a cache line, executing instructions with different power consumption. If the bit at a given offset of a secret byte is set, a fscale instruction is executed. Otherwise,  rdrand is executed. They assume an unprivileged attacker that  can trigger the code executed by the victim through an API  passing the offset to it.
In a second experiment, they mark pages of their victim as uncacheable. Thus, the code cannot leak through cache timings anymore. Still, with their power side-channel, they succeeded in observing the leakage.

column24:
A cross-core covert-channel is demonstrated with three similar variants using the RMW buffer, AIT buffer and read-write contention. 

Prime+Probe approach on the RMW buffer:
- Receiver accesses 8 locations to fill the RMW buffer with their data
- Sender reads from their file repeatedly to send 1, stays idle to send 0
- Receiver re-accesses their locations and measures the access latency
To avoid hits in the CPU cache, accesses during the probe step are offset by 64B compared to the prime step, taking advantage of the fact that RMW buffer lines are 256B. No details on how synchronization is achieved.

Prime+Probe approach on the AIT buffer:
- Receiver accesses 3 locations to fill the AIT buffer with their data
- Sender reads from their file repeatedly to send 1, stays idle to send 0
- Receiver re-accesses their locations and measures the access latency
To avoid hits in the CPU cache, accesses during the probe step are offset by 64B compared to the prime step, and to avoid hits in the RMW buffer, the receiver issues clflush instructions before priming their locations. No details on how synchronization is achieved.

Read-write contention on Optane memory modules:
- Receiver performs repeated random reads on their locations
- Sender repeatedly writes to a file to send 1 or stays idle to send 0
No details on how synchronization is achieved.

This remote covert-channel assumes both attacker have access to a server with an Optane-optimized key-value store. Internal counters used for wear-leveling are repurposed to transmit bits: 
- Sender continuously sends update requests to the store to send a value of 1, stays idle when sending 0
- Receiver continuously sends update requests and counts the number of requests needed before a wear-leveling event is measured by timing: a lower number indicates a 1 was sent.
No details as to how synchronization is achieved. An asynchronous variant is demonstrated where a whole message can be stored by the attacker on several blocks, and retrieved by the sender up to a day later.

The address owned by the sender, dubbed addrtrans, is used in the transmission stage. The receiver prepares an exclusive address (to be monitored by strex) which is L2congruent to addrtrans, and uses it along with the remaining eviction set to detect the sender’s accesses to addrtrans. To transmit 1 bit, the sender accesses addrtrans (and otherwise makes no access). The receiver pauses by iterating over a busy-waiting loop eight thousand times to give the sender sufficient time to access the targeted L2 set, and then checks whether addrtrans was accessed using strex.

column26:
First, they used 4-bit Berger codes to detect simple errors and to prevent zero from being a legal value in their protocol.

Then they did this the following techniques:
# Translation Caches:
1. Only flush the TLB (not the entire translation caches) to preserve useful cached entries.
2. Optimize page accesses to load only lower-level page tables into the Last-Level Cache (LLC).
3. Access pages in a sequential order to reduce unnecessary 2 MiB region accesses and maintain TLB effectiveness.
# Pointer Chasing:
1. Prevent hardware prefetching by using shuffled or pointer-chased access patterns (linked lists across cache lines).
2. Disable adaptive cache replacement noise by repeated forward and backward access to the eviction set.
# Re-using Physical Pages:
1. Use page coloring and address alignment to isolate eviction effects to page table entries (PTEs) only.
2. Preferred method: map one physical page to many page tables, so only page tables (not actual data pages) affect the cache set.
# Transactions (XLATE + ABORT):
1. Use Intel TSX to detect cache conflicts during hardware transactions.
2. Measure conflict events to determine if the target set was evicted, indicating activity by the victim.

column2: IChannels: Exploiting Current Management Mechanisms to Create Covert Channels in Modern Processors
column5: No evaluation for this conference this year
column16: Covert channel
column20: Compare to the numbers in previous papers
column24:
The IccThreadCovert covert channel communicates between a Sender and a Receiver running on the same hardware thread. The Sender encodes 2 secret bits, choosing a type of power-hungry instruction (PHI) from 4 intensity levels (L1-L4). Each level triggers an IPC (Instructions Per Cycle) throttling of varying duration, depending on the power required.

During this throttling, the Receiver executes a fixed loop (512b_Heavy) and measures how long it is slowed down (throttling period, TP). This TP varies according to the intensity of the PHI executed by the Sender, allowing the 2 transmitted bits to be decoded.

A pause of 650 µs (reset-time) is required between two transmissions, to allow the core voltage to return to baseline, otherwise the reading would be distorted by the hysteresis of the CPU voltage manager.
column26:
Described but not applied.

- An attacker can send the secret value many times and the Receiver can average the received value over a large number of measurements to obtain the secret value with an acceptable error rate.
- Use error detection and correction codes.
- The Sender/Receiver can initiate a communication transaction only during low-noise periods.
column33: Compare to the numbers in previous papers
column37:
The IccSMTcovert channel enables the Sender and Receiver, located on two SMT threads of the same physical core, to communicate via throttling side effects. The Sender encodes two secret bits by executing a PHI loop with an intensity (L1-L4) corresponding to the bits to be transmitted. This causes a global slowdown of the core, affecting both SMT threads: their IPC drops to 1/4 of the normal value.

The Receiver executes a simple loop (64b_loop) and measures its throttling period (TP), which depends on the power of the PHI executed by the Sender. By analyzing this period, it can decode the two bits transmitted.

As with IccThreadCovert, a waiting time of 650 µs is required between two transmissions for the core voltage to return to its base level.
column39:
Described but not applied.

- An attacker can send the secret value many times and the Receiver can average the received value over a large number of measurements to obtain the secret value with an acceptable error rate.
- Use error detection and correction codes.
- The Sender/Receiver can initiate a communication transaction only during low-noise periods.
column46: Compare to the numbers in previous papers
column50:
The IccCoresCovert channel enables the Sender and Receiver, running on two different physical cores, to communicate by exploiting the latency of voltage transitions caused by PHI instructions. The Sender encodes two bits by executing a PHI loop with an intensity (L1-L4) corresponding to the bits to be transmitted, which triggers a voltage increase on its core.

The Receiver also executes a PHI loop (128b_Heavy_loop), but is throttled waiting for the Sender's voltage rise to finish, as the regulator only handles one change at a time. The throttling period (TP), measured by the Receiver via rdtsc, depends on the PHI intensity of the Sender, allowing the two transmitted bits to be decoded.

Prior synchronization is required between the two threads to ensure that the transitions overlap correctly.

- receiver repeatedly times a nop loop: a performance increase indicate that the other thread is stalling (eg because of a cache miss) and so that a 1 is being sent

They use Google Chrome as their browser and the experiments are performed with Windows 11. They gather 600 traces from 40 popular videocontaining websites, and train with ATT-BLSTM (Attentionbased Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory).

Then they combined this attack with the one from another paper.They gather traces from the same 40 websites.They visit each website for 15 times and gather a 60-second-trace for each visit. They show how an attacker can learn secrets such as “model type” and “epoch time” of the training models in victim’s process. Then, they choose 12 LLM models and run each inference model for 60 times and gather 720 traces in total.
Afterward, they feed these traces into an ATT-BLSTM model, with hyperparameters being the same as those in the previous attack. With that, they achieve a classification accuracy of 93.75%. Thus, by gathering the PCI-e traffic trace, the attacker can guess which model the victim is using with high precision.

column2: NVLeak: Off-Chip Side-Channel Attacks via Non-Volatile Memory Systems
column5: Yes
column16: Covert channel;Side channel (includes (K)ASLR break, crypto attack, ...)
column24: Synchronizing: They implement a timing-based synchronization, where the sender and receiver use the same predefined time interval for each bit send/receive iteration: After the pointer-chasing operation finishes, the thread waits until the time interval is finished, then starts the next iteration.
column26: Avoiding noises due to cache prefetchers: There are multiple prefetchers for on-CPU caches and on-NVRAM caches in their system. These prefetchers cause latency fluctuations in the Prime+Probe accesses, adding noises to the channel. To defeat these prefetchers, they use our pointer chasing access pattern, which accesses all memory blocks, but in a random order so that prefetchers cannot detect a predictable pattern.
column37: Transmission: The sender program updates its file’s metadata at maximum frequency if sending bit 0; otherwise, it remains idle for a predetermined period to communicate bit 1. The receiver constantly updates its file at a low frequency to not trigger data migrations on its own but to detect that of the sender. The receiver also times these inode update functions and identifies the data migration as a long latency. The receiver picks up a bit 0 if it detects data migrations.

First, they tried to leak the SQL statements. They use the k-nearest neighbors algorithm (k-NN) with  Pearson correlation coefficients to analyze and categorize each access patterns quantitatively. To evaluate this categorization algorithm, they run each SQL operation 100 times, then for each operation, randomly choose 70 samples as the k-NN training set and use the rest 30 samples as the test set. In this evaluation, 240 SQL operations are tested and 202 of them are correctly categorized, resulting in an accuracy of 84%.
=> 24000 operation runs.

Then, they tried to leak the SQL range query. The queries have different memory access patterns in terms of access addresses and timings. They run each ranged query 100 times and use 70% of the results as training samples for the k-NN algorithm. In this evaluation, 120 queries are tested and 86 of them are correctly categorized, resulting in a 72% accuracy.
=> 120000 query runs.

column24:
This covert channel compares the parallel probing method with two variants of Prime+Scope. Sender and receiver reside in different Google Cloud Run instances on the same CPU. 

One thread repeatedly accesses a previously agreed upon Snoop Filter set on previously agreed timeslots. Another thread probes the set to detect if it was accessed. The latencies necessary to both prime and probe the set are recorded, as well as the detection rate of the techniques. No real message is actually transmitted.

In total 4070 measurements are used.
column55: Toy example / Microbenchmark
column57:
This micro-benchmark evaluates the effectiveness of address pruning algorithms for eviction set creation in noisy environments.

The authors compare the Group Testing algorithm and the Prime+Scope algorithm, optimized variants of both, and a Group Testing variant that further includes two novel optimizations. These optimizations are based on a filtering phase using L2 eviction set creation, then a binary search algorithm limiting the time needed for address pruning.

These algorithm are tested in two setups, a local cross-core one and a noisy, cross-tenant one running on Google Cloud Run, both setups use a different Skylake-SP processor model. Experiments are repeated in 3 different scenarios: building a single eviction set, eviction sets for a whole page, or for the whole system.
 
The success rate and time taken to build eviction sets is recorded. The experiments in the local setup were additionally repeated on an Ice Lake-SP CPU.

column2: BranchScope: A New Side-Channel Attack on Directional Branch Predictor
column5: No evaluation for this conference this year
column16: Covert channel
column24:
In one setup, sender and receiver are different processes running on different hyperthread of the same core. In a second setup, the sender resides in an SGX enclave.

To transmit a 1, the sender takes the branch, and does not take the branch to transmit a 0.

The receiver executes their corresponding branch two times, recording whether it was predicted correctly or not: if a sequence of two mispredicted branches, or one correctly predicted and one mispredicted one are observed, then a 1 was transmitted. Performance counters are used to determine whether a branch was mispredicted or not.

column2: Streamline: A Fast, Flushless Cache Covert-Channel Attack by Enabling Asynchronous Collusion
column5: Yes

column24:
Sender and receiver run on different cores and share a large array in memory for which they are assumed to have agreed on a traversal order. Both have also previously agreed on a seed for a PRNG.

When sending a bit, the sender XORs it with a bit obtained with the PRNG. If the resulting bit is 1, the sender accesses the current line and does not otherwise. The sender immediately moves to the next in the traversal order.

 Upon reaching this line, the receiver times its latency, and decodes its value, as they know the seed value of the PRNG. This mechanism ensures that a transmission contains on roughly the same number of 0 and 1.

Sender and receiver perform a coarse-grained synchronization every 200k cycles.
column26: Payloads are broken in to 8-byte packets, each appended with a (72,64) Hamming Code, correcting 1-bit errors and detecting 2-bit errors. This decreases the error rate to 0.12% with a bit rate of 1598 KB/s.

column24:
The sender can train the prefetcher on a previously agreed upon IP, encoding in the predicted stride the secret to be transmitted. By using either the Cache or PSC variant of AfterImage, the receiver can retrieve the encoded stride (authors seem to use the cache variant, through Flush+Reload). A 5-bit secret can thus be encoded.

No details is given as to how synchronization is done.
column55: Toy example / Microbenchmark
column57:
Authors employ the cache version of AfterImage on hand-crafted examples, demonstrating two scenarios: 

1) cross-thread/cross-process control flow leakage: demonstrated using Flush+Reload and Prime+Probe, with the target load's IP presumed to be known
2) cross user/kernel boundary control flow leakage: demonstrated using Flush+Reload, with the target load's IP retrieved through a brute-force like search technique

A similar toy example experiment is repeated on SGX.

Authors use the PSC variant of AfterImage to leak the secret exponent of an RSA decryption. The implementation used is a balanced Montgomery Ladder, written by the authors.

The prefetcher is mistrained by the attacker with a certain stride, then the victim executes. The attacker then re-executes the masking load and check whether the prefetcher was triggered. If it was, then the victim didn't execute its own load, ie it followed the else path.

column77: Authors shows how AfterImage can be used to augment other attacks by tracking the timing of a load. In particular, this can be used to track when an RSA key is loaded for decryption or when the AES S-box is loaded.

They also test 6 different keyboards and 6 smartphones running on different Adreno GPU/Android versions. They evaluate the impact of different CPU/GPU stress workloads and user input speed, and the time and memory overhead of inference.

They compare their work to a previous work on Nvidia GPUs that used different classification methods, on PC workloads.

column55: Other_JPEG decoder
column57:
The attack exploits an optimization commonly found in inverse cosine discrete transform implementations used in JPEG decoding. This transform is applied on 8*8 matrices to obtain 8*8 bitmap blocks, involving complex computations that can be skipped if all coefficients in rows/columns are zeroes except for the first element.

The implementation performs this check, and the authors use Pathfinder to retrieve the runtime control flow.
column58: 1000 branches recovered for simple images to 20k for high-res images
column61: Authors simply discuss the advantages of their method compared to previous papers
column67:
Authors test their Pathfinder algorithm on a set of toy examples, including different scenarios of loop iterations, nested loops and complex control flow as well as an AES encryption implementation.

This allows them to find limitations in their tool, in that it cannot tell the exact number of loop iterations after 194 iterations, and cannot go back too far in the branch history if it causes evictions in the PHT tables. However, they found these to not be a problem in later evaluations.
